ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for mochi-textured deep-fried donuts. FOODON_03315091 (doughnut) is the closest defensible match; mochi-texture and strawberry frosting are product-specific attributes not captured in FoodOn.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FKP covers doughnuts broadly; mochi-inspired chewy texture and strawberry frosting are product-specific and not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for mochi-style donut; SNOMED CT does not enumerate specific confectionery product variants at this level of specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: One Berry Mochimochi Donut weighs approximately 80 g and provides around 300 kcal, making it a high-calorie snack or dessert item.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Doughnuts, cake-type, plain (includes unsugared), enriched (SR Legacy ID 18247) and Doughnuts, yeast-leavened, glazed (SR Legacy ID 18249); values cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient database for fried dough confections
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Berry Mochimochi Donut exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore Foodo. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy doughnut entries (cake-type and glazed yeast-leavened) blended to reflect the mochi-texture (glutinous rice flour base) and strawberry frosting. Input record values (energy 375 kcal, protein 4.38 g, fat 22.5 g, carbohydrate 40.0 g, sugar 22.5 g, sodium 250 mg, dietary fibre 1.25 g, calcium 37.5 mg, iron 1.0 mg, cholesterol 25.0 mg, saturated fat 10.0 g, trans fat 0.63 g) are consistent with USDA doughnut reference ranges and are retained as the best available estimate. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, potassium, and micronutrients not reported in the input are set to -1 (data not available from adapted source).

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Doughnut, cake type (GI 76, glucose reference)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No GI value exists specifically for a mochi-style strawberry-frosted deep-fried donut. GI 76 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cake-type doughnut (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care 31:2281), which is the closest approved analogous food. The mochi-glutinous rice flour base may slightly elevate GI relative to standard wheat-flour doughnuts; GI 76 is therefore a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (76 × 32.0) / 100 = 24.3 (available carbohydrate per 80 g serving = 40.0 × 0.80 = 32.0 g).

hypertension.key_factor: High sugar (22.5 g/100 g), high saturated fat (10.0 g/100 g), and elevated sodium (250 mg/100 g) collectively promote vascular stress and insulin-mediated sodium retention.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid this food; its combination of high sugar, saturated fat, and sodium exceeds safe thresholds for blood pressure management and contributes to arterial stiffness and fluid retention.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar at 22.5 g/100 g exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, total carbohydrate is 40.0 g/100 g with only 1.25 g/100 g dietary fibre, and the estimated GI of 76 produces a high glycaemic load of 24.3 per serving.
type2_diabetes.guidance: This food is contraindicated for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes; the high sugar content, refined carbohydrate load, and high glycaemic index will cause rapid postprandial blood glucose elevation and should be avoided entirely.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat at 10.0 g/100 g (double the AHA 5 g/100 g threshold), trans fat at 0.63 g/100 g, and high sugar all contribute to LDL elevation, endothelial dysfunction, and atherogenic risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this food; the saturated fat and trans fat content from deep-frying, combined with high sugar, directly elevates LDL cholesterol and promotes atherosclerotic plaque formation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat at 10.0 g/100 g and sugar at 22.5 g/100 g both exceed RA pro-inflammatory thresholds.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this food; AGEs from deep-frying, combined with high saturated fat and sugar, promote pro-inflammatory cytokine production and worsen joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Berry Mochimochi Donut is a deep-fried confection sold in Singapore, inspired by the chewy, elastic texture of Japanese mochi (glutinous rice cake). The dough incorporates glutinous rice flour alongside wheat flour to achieve a distinctively dense and springy crumb that differentiates it from conventional Western-style doughnuts. After deep-frying, the donut is coated with a strawberry-flavored frosting, giving it a sweet, fruity glaze and a vibrant pink appearance. The result is a high-calorie, ultra-processed snack with 375 kcal per 100 g, 22.5 g fat (of which 10.0 g is saturated fat and 0.63 g is trans fat), 40.0 g carbohydrate, and 22.5 g sugar. Dietary fibre is low at 1.25 g/100 g, and sodium is elevated at 250 mg/100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is 76 (high), producing a glycaemic load of approximately 24.3 per 80 g serving. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar, saturated fat, trans fat, and refined carbohydrate content. It is vegetarian but not vegan, and contains gluten, dairy, and egg allergens. It is commonly consumed as a dessert or snack in Singapore's café and donut-shop culture.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Berry Mochimochi Donut exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy doughnut entries and are consistent with the input record values, which originate from GrabFood (a delivery platform, not an approved primary source). Confidence is medium because the adapted USDA doughnut reference is a defensible analogue but does not capture the specific glutinous rice flour formulation or the exact frosting composition of this product.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this mochi-style donut product is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy doughnut entries or the Singapore HPB Foodo database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this product is not reported in the adapted USDA doughnut source. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this specific product is not available in the adapted source. 4. All vitamin fields (vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg) are set to -1: micronutrient data for this specific mochi-style donut product is not available in the adapted USDA source at the required level of specificity. 5. phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg are set to -1 for the same reason. 6. The original input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was listed under the HPB Singapore organization but the URL does not correspond to the official HPB Foodo nutrient database; nutrient values have been validated against USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy doughnut entries instead. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.63 g/100 g is retained from the input record; this is plausible for a commercially deep-fried product using partially hydrogenated oils, consistent with USDA doughnut reference data. 8. No zero-value core macros are present; the zero-macro justification rule (g4) is not triggered.
